questions.question: S3 Standard is designed for **11 nines** of durability. What does that 99.999999999% figure actually describe?
options: The probability that you can reach an object at any given moment
The probability that an object survives over a year without being lost
The percentage of requests that return within the SLA latency target
The fraction of time the S3 API endpoint is reachable each year
questions.explanation: Durability is the probability your object survives (data is not lost). Reachability 'right now' is availability, which is lower (Standard targets 99.99%) and class-dependent — conflating the two is the most common S3 interview slip.

questions.question: Since December 2020, a successful overwrite-PUT to S3 is immediately visible to any subsequent GET, with no eventual-consistency window — and this requires no extra configuration or cost.
questions.explanation: S3 has provided strong read-after-write consistency for all operations (PUTs, overwrites, DELETEs, and LISTs) since December 2020, at no extra cost or config. The old eventual-consistency caveat is a myth you must bust.

questions.question: What is the maximum size of a single S3 object, and the cap on a single PUT?
options: 5 GB object maximum; 5 GB single-PUT cap
5 TB object maximum; 5 GB single-PUT cap
5 TB object maximum; 5 TB single-PUT cap
5 GB object maximum; 100 MB single-PUT cap
questions.explanation: An object can be up to 5 TB, but a single PUT is capped at 5 GB. Above 5 GB you must use multipart upload (recommended above ~100 MB for throughput and resilience).

questions.question: In the key `reports/2026/q2.csv`, the slashes create real nested folders on disk that S3 stores hierarchically.
questions.explanation: The S3 namespace is flat — the key is one opaque UTF-8 string and the slashes are just convention. The console renders 'folders' by treating `/` as a delimiter, but nothing is nested on disk; the prefix is used only for listing, lifecycle, and replication scoping.

questions.question: If you PUT a new object to a modern S3 bucket without specifying any encryption settings, what happens?
options: The object is stored unencrypted until you enable default encryption
The PUT is rejected because encryption is mandatory but unspecified
The object is encrypted automatically with at least SSE-S3
The object is encrypted only if Block Public Access is enabled
questions.explanation: Encryption at rest is on by default — every new object is encrypted with at least SSE-S3 automatically, and you cannot store unencrypted objects. No configuration is required for baseline encryption.

questions.question: An object's access pattern is unknown and likely to change over time, and you want to avoid retrieval fees and managing transition rules. Which storage class fits best?
options: Standard-IA
One Zone-IA
Intelligent-Tiering
Glacier Instant Retrieval
questions.explanation: Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between access tiers based on observed access, has no retrieval fees (just a small per-object monitoring fee), and needs no lifecycle rule — the 'set it and forget it' choice for unknown/changing patterns. Standard-IA and Glacier classes charge retrieval fees and assume a known pattern.

questions.question: How does **One Zone-IA** differ from the other standard storage classes with respect to data loss risk?
options: It has lower designed durability because it keeps one copy in a single AZ; losing that AZ loses the data
It has the same multi-AZ durability but simply a lower availability target
It replicates across two AZs instead of three, halving durability
It stores no redundant copies at all, so any disk failure loses data
questions.explanation: Unlike the other classes that share 11-nines durability across at least three AZs, One Zone-IA keeps its copy in a single AZ — lose the AZ, lose the data. That is why it is ~20% cheaper and reserved for re-creatable data.

questions.question: On a versioning-enabled bucket, you issue a `DELETE` on key `data.json` without specifying a version ID. What happens, and how do you restore the object?
options: The latest version is permanently erased; restore from a backup or replica
A delete marker is added as the new latest version (GETs return 404); restore by deleting the marker
All versions are removed at once; restore by re-enabling versioning
The delete is rejected because a version ID is required on a versioned bucket
questions.explanation: A versioned DELETE without a version ID does not erase data — it adds a zero-byte delete marker that becomes the latest version, so GETs return 404. You recover by deleting the marker. To truly remove a version you must DELETE with its specific versionId.

questions.question: A bucket in account A must be readable by a role in partner account B, but never public. What is required for the cross-account grant to work?
options: Only a bucket policy in account A naming account B's role as principal
Only an IAM policy on account B's role allowing the S3 actions
An allow on both sides: a bucket policy in A naming B's role and an IAM policy on B's role
An ACL on each object setting `bucket-owner-full-control` for account B
questions.explanation: Cross-account access requires an allow on both sides — the resource (bucket) policy in account A AND the identity (IAM) policy on the principal in account B. A single-side allow is insufficient across accounts. ACLs are disabled by default and are not the mechanism here.

questions.question: An SSE-KMS bucket serving millions of small reads per hour has a surprisingly large **KMS** bill. What is the correct fix that preserves CloudTrail audit and key rotation?
options: Switch the bucket to SSE-S3 to eliminate all KMS calls
Enable S3 Bucket Keys so a bucket-level data key envelope-encrypts many objects
Move the objects to Glacier Deep Archive to reduce request volume
Disable CloudTrail logging of KMS GenerateDataKey calls
questions.explanation: S3 Bucket Keys reuse a short-lived bucket-level data key to envelope-encrypt many objects, collapsing thousands of per-object GenerateDataKey/Decrypt calls into far fewer — cutting KMS cost while keeping the CMK, its policy, rotation, and CloudTrail audit. Switching to SSE-S3 would lose the audit trail.

questions.question: A backend generates a presigned PUT URL using a role that only has `s3:GetObject` (not `s3:PutObject`). A client uses the URL to upload. What happens?
options: The upload succeeds because the presigned URL itself grants the PUT permission
The upload fails because the URL cannot grant more than the signer's own permissions
The upload succeeds but the object is quarantined until an admin approves it
The upload succeeds only if Block Public Access is turned off
questions.explanation: A presigned URL embeds a signature derived from the signer's permissions and cannot grant more than the signer holds. If the signing role lacks s3:PutObject, the presigned PUT fails — the URL conveys the signer's authority, not new authority.

questions.question: Given the .NET code below, which statement is correct?
options: TransferUtility always uses a single PUT regardless of file size
TransferUtility automatically switches to multipart with parallel parts for large files, handling part sizing and retries
This code fails for files over 100 MB unless you call InitiateMultipartUpload first
TransferUtility disables retries, so a failed part restarts the whole upload
questions.explanation: The high-level TransferUtility auto-switches to multipart and parallel parts for large files, handling part sizing, parallelism, and retries — which is why it is preferred over hand-rolling the part API. A failed part retries without restarting the whole upload.

questions.question: S3 event notifications are at-least-once and not guaranteed to be strictly ordered, so consumers should be written to be idempotent.
questions.explanation: S3 notifications deliver at-least-once and without strict ordering guarantees, so a consumer can see duplicates or out-of-order events — making idempotency a requirement for correct downstream processing.

questions.question: What does this bucket policy statement enforce?
options: It denies all S3 actions on the bucket whenever the request does not use TLS (HTTPS)
It allows public read access only over HTTPS
It blocks all access to the bucket from every principal at all times
It denies requests that lack a KMS encryption header
questions.explanation: The condition `aws:SecureTransport=false` matches non-TLS requests, and the Deny on `s3:*` for both the bucket and its objects (`/*`) forces all access over HTTPS. It does not grant anything (it is a Deny) and is unrelated to encryption-header enforcement.

questions.question: Regulators require audit logs to be immutable for 7 years such that **not even the root user** can delete them early. Which configuration is correct?
options: Object Lock in Governance mode with a 7-year retention
Object Lock in Compliance mode with a 7-year retention
A Legal Hold with MFA Delete enabled
Versioning plus a lifecycle rule that expires objects after 7 years
questions.explanation: Only Object Lock Compliance mode prevents anyone — including the root account — from shortening or removing the retention until it expires. Governance mode allows holders of s3:BypassGovernanceRetention to override it, so it fails the 'not even root' requirement.

questions.question: Your application needs far more than 5,500 GET/s against a single bucket. What is the correct way to scale request throughput?
options: Request an account-level bucket request-rate quota increase from AWS Support
Spread keys across many prefixes, since the ~5,500 GET/s and ~3,500 write/s limits are per prefix and prefixes are unlimited
Shard the data across multiple buckets because the limit is per bucket
Enable Transfer Acceleration, which removes the per-bucket request-rate ceiling
questions.explanation: The rate limits (~3,500 writes/s and ~5,500 GET/HEAD per second) are per prefix, not per bucket, and a bucket has no limit on the number of prefixes. You scale horizontally by spreading keys across more prefixes. Transfer Acceleration speeds long-distance transfers but does not raise the request-rate unit.

questions.question: Within a single AWS account, which statements about how an S3 request is authorized are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: An explicit Deny in any IAM or bucket policy overrides any Allow
Access can be granted if either an identity policy or the resource policy allows it
Block Public Access can override a policy/ACL that would grant public access
If neither an identity policy nor a bucket policy allows the action, it is granted by default
An object ACL set to `public-read` reliably grants public access on a modern bucket
questions.explanation: An explicit Deny always wins; within an account either an identity or resource policy allowing the action suffices; and BPA overrides public-granting policies/ACLs. Absent any allow, access is an implicit Deny (not granted). With ACLs disabled by default (Bucket owner enforced), a `public-read` ACL fails or is meaningless.

questions.question: Which statements about S3 replication and serving a private bucket through CloudFront are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Replication (CRR or SRR) requires versioning enabled on both the source and destination buckets
Delete markers are replicated automatically by default
Origin Access Control (OAC) is preferred over the legacy OAI and supports SSE-KMS origins
Replication needs an IAM role that S3 assumes to read the source and write the destination
Same-Region Replication (SRR) places the destination in a different Region
questions.explanation: Replication requires versioning on both buckets and an IAM role S3 assumes to read source and write destination; OAC replaces OAI, supports SSE-KMS origins and SigV4, and is the modern choice. Delete-marker replication is opt-in (not automatic), and SRR keeps the destination in the SAME Region (CRR is the cross-Region one).
